sanskrit_devanagari:
यततो ह्यपि कौन्तेय पुरुषस्य विपश्चितः ।
इन्द्रियाणि प्रमाथीनि हरन्ति प्रसभं मनः ॥ ६० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yatato hy api kaunteya puruṣasya vipaścitaḥ |
indriyāṇi pramāthīni haranti prasabhaṁ manaḥ || 60 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; uses single danda | as terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.60।। rather than ॥ ६० ॥)
IAST exact (Devanagari rendered without transliteration marks; IAST body matches: 'yatato hy api kaunteya purusasya vipascitah indriyani pramathini haranti prasabham manah')

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.060 = MBh_06,024.060). Body text identical: 'yatato hy api kaunteya puruṣasya vipaścitaḥ / indriyāṇi pramāthīni haranti prasabhaṃ manaḥ'. GRETIL writes 'prasabhaṃ' with anusvara where vedabase prints 'prasabhaṁ' (candrabindu); same Sanskrit conjunct under different scholarly conventions.

anvaya.meaning: of the wise / of one of discriminating knowledge
they carry away / they snatch away

translations.verbatim_quote: The senses are so strong and impetuous, O Arjuna, that they forcibly carry away the mind even of a man of discrimination who is endeavoring to control them.
The senses are so strong and turbulent, O son of Kunti, that they can forcibly carry away the mind even of a person endowed with discrimination who practices self-control.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There are many learned sages, philosophers and transcendentalists who try to conquer the senses, but in spite of their endeavors, even the greatest of them sometimes fall victim to material sense enjoyment due to the agitated mind.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Shankara bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Hi, for; kaunteya, O son of Kunti; pramathini, the turbulent; indriyani, organs; prasabham, violently; haranti, snatch away; manah, the mind; vipascitah, of an intelligent; purusasya, person; api, even; yatatah, while he is striving diligently. Indeed, the organs confound a person who is inclined towards objects, and after confounding him, violently carry away his mind endowed with discriminating knowledge, even when he is aware of this.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as a sober warning against premature confidence in self-control. Even the discriminating one who is actively striving (yatataḥ) can have the mind snatched away by the senses when those senses are oriented toward objects. The condition for being immune is not striving alone but the conjunction of striving with the deeper non-orientation of the mind (treated in 2.61). This verse's role is to refuse the assumption that effort by itself secures the result.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: summary-only (raw bgus rendering for this verse duplicates the Shankara excerpt; Ramanuja's distinct text not machine-extractable from this source)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya on 2.60 reads the verse as the diagnostic that motivates the prescription in 2.61. Sense-control by the unaided striving will of the embodied person is insufficient because the senses, when oriented toward objects (viṣaya), are turbulent (pramāthīni) and can violently (prasabham) carry away the mind even of one with discriminating knowledge (vipaścitaḥ). The structural argument: there is a mutual dependence between sense-control and direct vision of the self, and this dependence cannot be broken by effort alone — hence 2.61's instruction to fix the mind on the Supreme as the auspicious object of meditation, which is what releases the will from this circular trap.

positions.rendering: even of a man of discrimination who is endeavoring to control them (api modifies the discriminating one who is striving)
even of a person endowed with discrimination who practices self-control
two readings noted: api may be construed either with yatataḥ (even while he is striving) or with vipaścitaḥ puruṣasya (even of an intelligent person)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Shankara's translator (Gambhirananda) explicitly flags the syntactic ambiguity in api: it can be construed either with yatataḥ ('even while striving') or with vipaścitaḥ puruṣasya ('even an intelligent person'). The two readings are not contradictory — they amplify each other; the verse warns that both striving and discrimination can fail. Modern translators (Prabhupada, Mukundananda) collapse the ambiguity into a single phrase. The lexical force is the same; the grammatical option is preserved by Shankara for completeness.

All four readings cluster around an intensity quality of the senses: turbulent, agitating, churning, impetuous. The Sanskrit root math- carries a churning/agitating force (compare amṛta-manthana, the churning of the ocean). The verse's claim is not that the senses are merely 'strong' in a passive sense but that they actively churn the mind from its established posture — they do agitative work on the operator's interior. The translations differ in how strongly they preserve the active churning sense; Shankara's 'turbulent' and Vishvanatha's 'causing agitation' retain the active force most explicitly.

literal_meaning: For even of a striving discriminating person, O son of Kuntī, the violent senses forcibly carry away the mind.
traditional_meaning_consensus: This verse is the warning that immediately precedes Krishna's prescription in 2.61. Even the wise person (vipaścitaḥ) who is actively striving (yatataḥ) for self-control is vulnerable: the senses, when turbulent (pramāthīni) and oriented toward sense-objects, can violently (prasabham) carry away the mind. Shankara reads this as the sober refusal of any assumption that effort alone secures the result; Ramanuja reads it as the structural diagnosis that motivates fixing the mind on the Supreme as the auspicious object of meditation (the prescription in 2.61); the Gaudiya Vaishnava reading folds in the practical observation that even great sages have fallen due to agitation of the mind. All traditions agree: discipline is ongoing, not achieved. The wise are not exempt from vigilance.

remaining_gaps: Ramanuja's distinct 2.60 commentary not machine-extractable from bgus (page renders Shankara content under both slots); paraphrase from authoritative summary used.
Shankara's full Sanskrit bhashya not pulled — only translator-rendered (Gambhirananda) excerpt was machine-extractable.
